title: Gouy phase engineering of self-splitting quantum correlations
authors: A. L. S. Santos Junior, M. Damaceno, A. C. Barbosa, N. A. Ribeiro, G. B. Alves, P. H. Souto Ribeiro, A. Z. Khoury
abstract: In this work, we demonstrate the effect of self-splitting spatial quantum correlations induced by Gouy phase engineering. In the process of spontaneous parametric down conversion the pump beam is structured with a mode superposition that produces a dynamical splitting and recombination of the light beam. This structure is transferred to the quantum correlations between signal and idler photons. As a result the joint two-photon probability distribution propagates like a self-splitting and recombining light beam, implementing a Mach-Zehnder-like interferometer. We observe heralded single-photon interference and two-photon NOON state interference. These results open new avenues for applications in quantum metrology.
summary: This paper demonstrates how the Gouy phase of a structured pump beam can be used to engineer the spatial correlations of photon pairs produced via SPDC. By manipulating the pump beam's modes, the researchers created a 'self-splitting' quantum correlation that acts as a built-in Mach-Zehnder interferometer. They successfully observed heralded single-photon interference and two-photon NOON state interference, even achieving super-resolution. This technique provides a new way to control quantum light propagation and develop robust quantum metrology applications.
main_problem: Investigating how the spatial structure and propagation dynamics of a structured pump beam can be transferred to quantum-correlated photon pairs via Gouy phase engineering.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated that the pump beam's self-splitting and recombination dynamics are imprinted onto the photon correlations, creating a Mach-Zehnder-like interferometer that enables heralded single-photon interference, two-photon NOON state interference, and super-resolution.
method: Using Spontaneous Parametric Down-Conversion (SPDC) in a PPKTP crystal, the pump beam is shaped with a Spatial Light Modulator (SLM) into Hermite-Gauss mode superpositions, and the resulting photon correlations are analyzed via 2D coincidence scanning.
model_or_system: An experimental platform consisting of a 405 nm CW laser, a PPKPT crystal for SPDC, and an SLM to prepare structured modes; the system utilizes the Gouy phase of Hermite-Gauss modes to engineer the spatial evolution of the biphoton state.
key_observables: Coincidence counting rate, heralded single-photon interference patterns, two-photon NOON state interference, and spatial fringe spacing.
important_parameters: Control phase (theta_c), Rayleigh length (z_R), beam width (w(z)), and the two-photon de Broglie wavelength.
main_assumptions: Paraxial approximation for light propagation, thin crystal approximation for the SPDC process, and the assumption of full transfer of the pump's angular spectrum to the two-photon state.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the transverse intensity patterns of the self-splitting beam; Figure 2 depicts the experimental setup; Figure 3 displays 2D scans of heralded single-photon patterns; Figure 7 shows the far-field interference of the pump beam and the spatial coincidence distribution of down-converted photons.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of Gouy phase engineering, describes the experimental setup and mode preparation, presents the results of the transfer of spatial structure to photon pairs, demonstrates the implementation of a spatial interferometer with super-resolution, and concludes with the implications for quantum metrology.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information as it demonstrates a novel method for engineering complex spatial quantum states and implementing interferometric protocols (like NOON state sensing) using the intrinsic propagation dynamics of structured light.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Self-splitting beam dynamics under free propagation. Transverse intensity of the superposition given by Eq.(3) as a function of ˜z for (a) θc = 0 and (b) θc = π. The upper axis shows the one-to-one mapping between z and the control phase θc applied at ˜z = 0 in (a) and at ˜z = 5 in (b).

FIG. 2. Experimental setup. A 405 nm CW laser is shaped by a spatial light modulator (SLM) and imaged onto a non- linear crystal, generating photon pairs at 780 nm (signal) and 840 nm (idler) via SPDC. A dichroic mirror (DM) separates signal and idler photons and sends them to single-photon de- tection in coincidence (CC). Two configurations are used: in the heralded one, only the signal coupler is scanned; in the biphoton one, both couplers are scanned synchronously. In the biphoton configuration a glass plate (GP) is inserted to demonstrate phase sensitivity improvement.

FIG. 3. Experimental characterization of the self-splitting quantum spatial correlation at the heralded single-photon level. The pump beam is prepared as the self-splitting su- perposition Ψ at ˜z = 0. Panels (a), (b), and (c) show the coincidence counts resolved over the transverse scan position (x, y) in the detection plane located at ˜z = 5 for pump rela- tive phases θc = 0, π/2, and π, respectively.

FIG. 4. Coincidence counts as a function of transverse x posi- tion. (a) Without obstacle: the HG00 mode (purple squares) fitted by a Gaussian profile (dotted curve), and the superpo- sition state (blue circles) fitted with the dashed curve. (b) With an obstacle (≈1.2 mm slit), the same theoretical pro- files in (a) are overlaid as a reference.

FIG. 5. Experimental emulation of the self-splitting effect by tuning θc. Varying θc reproduces the effective longitudinal evolution along the optical axis discussed in the simulation of Fig. 1. Panels (a) and (b) show the coincidence counts from an xz-emulated scan of the signal arm for two detec- tion configurations: (a) the heralded single-photon, with the idler fixed at xi = 0, and (b) the biphoton state, obtained by jointly scanning both detectors with xs = xi. Panel (c) shows the single-photon counts measured with an attenuated pump under the same scan conditions as (a).

FIG. 6. Self-splitting biphoton distribution for θc = 0 (blue circles) and θc = π (black squares). Panel (a) shows the standard joint-scan configuration (xi = xs), while panel (b) shows the counter-scan configuration (xi = −xs).

FIG. 7. Phase-sensing in the self-splitting interferometer. (a) Far-field interference of the pump beam (405 nm) after inserting a glass plate in the left near-field peak. (b) Spatial coincidence distribution of the down-converted photons for two detection configurations: idler fixed at xi = 0 (heralded single-photon) and joint detection (xi = xs).
